doom.driver: US strikes Iran in response to a drone attack on a ship. The U.S. struck Iran on Friday in response to a drone attack a day earlier on a cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz. It's the most significant test yet to an interim understanding reached a week ago by
doom.summary: The US-Iran 'interim understanding' — a diplomatic euphemism for 'please stop shooting at each other' — lasted approximately one week before both sides were trading strikes again. Iran says the US violated their deal; the US says Iran violated it first; the IRGC is now threatening 'broader' future responses, which is exactly the kind of escalatory language that makes geopolitical analysts reach for antacids. Meanwhile, the government model restrictions story (US deciding who gets GPT-5.6) adds a quietly unsettling AI governance subplot to an already messy Friday.

factors.name: Geopolitics
factors.detail: US and Iran are trading direct military strikes while simultaneously blaming each other for breaking a ceasefire — a fragile arrangement that now looks like it's unraveling in real time.

factors.name: AI Capability
factors.detail: The US government is gatekeeping access to GPT-5.6 and Anthropic's Mythos, signaling that frontier AI is now officially a strategic national security asset with restricted distribution.

factors.name: Governance
factors.detail: OpenAI limiting model access to 'trusted partners' at US government request represents an unprecedented fusion of state power and AI deployment decisions.

factors.name: Alignment
factors.detail: No major alignment-specific developments today; the big AI news is about access control rather than capability safety.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: A plane crashing into Beijing's tallest building with unverified cause and no official statement is the kind of ambiguous incident that could mean nothing — or a great deal more.

doom.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing — interior scene, camera positioned inside a cramped Omani fishing family's concrete home on the Musandam peninsula looking outward through a single salt-crusted window, deep navy and smoldering orange palette, the window frame filling most of the composition with its peeling white paint and rusted iron latch, outside the window the Strait of Hormuz stretches across the middle distance rendered in threatening deep blue-green, the narrow water channel choked with the dark silhouettes of dozens of anchored supertankers sitting motionless on the horizon like a city of black monoliths, the far Iranian coastline visible as a dark brown mountain ridge beneath a sky smeared orange-amber with smoke haze, inside the room a small battery-powered radio sits on a cracked concrete sill casting faint amber light upward, beside it a glass of tea gone cold, the walls of the room are bare rough concrete with a hanging calendar page fluttering slightly, on the window glass a child's handprint smudge catches the orange glow from outside, the entire outdoor scene filtered through the dirty glass giving the tankers and smoky horizon a ghostly distorted quality, heavy shadows pool in the room's corners, warmth from outside feels like threat not comfort, heat shimmer visible above the water between the stranded ships.

dawn.driver: Ancient human DNA found on cave art for the first time. DNA from ancient humans has been found on a prehistoric cave painting and on cave walls, demonstrating the potential to one day identify individual artists and resolve the debate over Neanderthals' artistic contribution.
dawn.blurb: WE FOUND THE ARTIST BEHIND THE ANCIENT HAND
dawn.summary: In a week scarred by earthquakes, airstrikes, and a heatwave breaking German records, science quietly delivered something extraordinary: ancient human DNA recovered directly from prehistoric cave paintings, connecting us across tens of thousands of years to the hands that made them. This isn't just archaeology — it's the potential to finally answer whether Neanderthals were artists, to put individual names (or at least genetic identities) to the people who first felt the urge to create. On days when humanity seems determined to break itself apart, it's worth pausing on the fact that we're also the species that painted caves, wondered who painted them, and then figured out how to ask the rock itself.

factors.name: Science
factors.detail: Recovering readable ancient human DNA from cave art surfaces is a methodological breakthrough that opens an entirely new field of archaeogenetic investigation.

factors.name: Cooperation
factors.detail: Israel and Lebanon signed a US-brokered framework agreement, a fragile but real diplomatic signal amid an extraordinarily volatile regional moment.

factors.name: Human Spirit
factors.detail: International rescue teams poured into Venezuela within 48 hours of the earthquakes, with China, the US, and regional neighbors all sending aid in a rare moment of convergent goodwill.

factors.name: Environment
factors.detail: California's ban on obnoxiously loud streaming ads — a small but genuine quality-of-life win — takes effect July 1, with Illinois following, showing consumer protection governance still functions.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: African soccer teams are outperforming expectations at the World Cup, with Cape Verde making history and Egypt advancing, signaling a genuine shift in the global game's power balance.

dawn.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing. Wide aerial view looking down at a 45-degree angle, vast landscape dominates, tiny figures below. A limestone karst plateau in the Iberian Peninsula stretches across the entire frame, golden-amber late light raking across pale grey-beige rock at a low angle, casting long teal shadows into every crevice and hollow, the terrain ancient and pitted and enormous. Far below, scattered across the plateau like seeds, tiny crowds of people have gathered outside a dozen cave entrances — dark oval mouths in the rock face, each one glowing faintly warm from within, amber light bleeding out from deep inside the earth. The cave openings are the emotional centers of the image, small but radiating, like embers set into stone. Thin trails of people wind across the landscape toward these entrances, miniature figures dwarfed by the immensity of rock and time. A few figures crouch at a cave mouth examining something on the ground, invisible at this scale but purposeful. The ochre-red of the plateau's iron-rich soil glows warmly against deep teal pooling in the valleys and shadow-crevices. The sky is a deep radiant amber-teal gradient, long horizontal clouds catching last light. The whole world feels ancient, breathing, and newly cracked open.
